one_line_takeaway: AttnGCG shows that steering an aligned LLM's attention onto the adversarial suffix (via an added attention-maximization loss on top of GCG) both boosts jailbreak success (avg ASRGPT 64.3%->70.6%) and offers an interpretable account of why suffix attacks work, though it stays white-box and collapses on post-2024 frontier models.
key_finding: Adding attention loss consistently raises jailbreak success. Direct white-box attack: average ASRGPT rises from 64.3% (GCG) to 70.6% (AttnGCG), +6.3 points, and ASRKW from 74.1% to 78.0%. The gain is a genuine reduction in 'false jailbreaks' (affirmative-then-refuse): on Gemma models the ASRKW-vs-ASRGPT gap shrinks from 23.5% to 15.5%. Mechanistically, AttnGCG lowers goal-attention from 4.56e-2 (GCG) to 4.17e-2 (8.6% lower). It generalizes as an add-on and transfers markedly better across unseen goals (held-out Test ASRGPT 80.3% -> 91.7%, +11.4 points) than GCG.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is AttnGCG, an enhancement of the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) jailbreak that adds an auxiliary 'attention loss' to the optimization objective. Building on the paper's own observation that a rising attention score on the adversarial suffix (and a falling score on the system/goal prompts) strongly correlates with jailbreak success, AttnGCG augments GCG's target-token loss Lt with an attention loss La that directly maximizes the average last-layer attention paid to the suffix tokens over the target output tokens. The combined objective Lt+a = wt*Lt + wa*La is optimized with the same greedy-coordinate search as GCG (Algorithm 1). Lt is retained as a regulator so goal-attention does not collapse so low that the model produces affirmative-but-irrelevant text. GCG, AutoDAN, ICA, PGD, PAIR etc. are cited prior work, not this paper's contribution; AttnGCG is positioned as a drop-in objective add-on to GCG-style attacks.
threat_model: White-box optimization-based jailbreak: the adversary has access to model internals (attention weights and gradients) of an open-weight aligned chat LLM and optimizes a token-level adversarial suffix appended to a harmful goal prompt, aiming to override the safety-aligned system prompt and elicit prohibited content. The paper further studies two transfer settings where the white-box-optimized suffix is reused (a) against unseen harmful goals on the same model and (b) against black-box closed-weight models (GPT-3.5 variants, GPT-4-1106, Gemini-Pro) to which the attacker has only query access.
limitations: Purely white-box for the core attack (requires attention/gradient access); transfer to closed models is weak in absolute terms (avg ASRGPT only 45.5%, and on GPT-4-1106 just ~4%). The authors' own stated limitation: the attack essentially fails on models released after ~Jan 2024 (Gemini-1.5-Pro-latest, Gemini-1.5-Flash, GPT-4o), e.g. ~0.5% GCG / ~1% AttnGCG on Gemini-1.5-Flash, so conclusions there are not representative. Evaluation is on 100 (or 25+100) AdvBench behaviors, uses a GPT-4 judge as ground truth, and improvements over GCG are often small single-digit points on already-vulnerable models (e.g. Mistral-7B +1% ASRGPT).

datasets_used: AdvBench Harmful Behaviors (Zou et al. 2023) — 520 requests; 100 randomly sampled for direct-attack evaluation
Transfer-across-goals split: 25 harmful goals for universal-suffix training + 100 held-out goals for Test ASR
HarmBench referenced for selecting AutoDAN as a strong baseline
quantitative_results: Direct attack average ASRGPT: GCG 64.3% -> AttnGCG 70.6% (+6.3)
Direct attack average ASRKW: GCG 74.1% -> AttnGCG 78.0% (+3.9)
Goal attention score (Table 2): Init 5.84e-2, GCG 4.56e-2, AttnGCG 4.17e-2 (8.6% drop vs GCG)
AutoDAN+AttnGCG ASRGPT 91.0% vs AutoDAN+GCG 86.0% vs AutoDAN alone 35.0% (Llama-2-7B)
ICA+AttnGCG ASRGPT 61.0% vs ICA+GCG 56.0% vs ICA alone 0.0%
Transfer across goals held-out Test ASRGPT: GCG 80.3% -> AttnGCG 91.7% (+11.4)
Transfer across models average ASRGPT: GCG 42.7% -> AttnGCG 45.5% (+2.8); ASRKW 49.1% -> 51.5%
Runtime: GCG ~75 min for 500 steps; common-prefix KV-cache trick gives ~5x speedup
baselines_compared: GCG (Zou et al. 2023) — primary baseline for both direct and transfer attacks
AutoDAN (Liu et al. 2024) — as a base method AttnGCG is layered onto
ICA / in-context attack (Wei et al. 2024) — optimization-free base method
